text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 30 May 2016 

Dr Anita Kerr
Riverside Medical Centre

22 Riverside Walk, London, SE10 9AB
Dear Dr Anita Kerr
Michael Carter, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: H837294 NHS No. 7845632190
Flat 3, 14 Millstream Court, London, SE10 8PT 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. As you know he has generalised epilepsy. Since my last assessment he reports a variable pattern of episodes but overall a frequency of ≤ four seizures per week, typically brief generalised convulsions or absence episodes as described below. He has maintained good adherence to medication and has been keeping a diary which corroborates this frequency.

He describes two main event types: (1) abrupt loss of awareness with eyelid fluttering and brief behavioural arrest lasting 10–20 seconds, occurring in clusters on stressful days; and (2) generalised tonic–clonic seizures, usually from wakefulness, lasting around 60–90 seconds with post‑ictal fatigue and myalgia. There have been no focal warning symptoms, tongue biting is occasional, and injuries have been minor (one bruised shoulder last month). There is no clear menstrual or sleep‑deprivation trigger; however, he notes that missed meals and high caffeine intake may precipitate events. Family members have witnessed several episodes and confirm generalised onset without lateralising features.

Past antiseizure medication side effects have been documented historically, including a pruritic rash with Lamotrigine (resolved on cessation) and gastrointestinal upset with Valproate (nausea and dyspepsia at higher doses). He is currently taking Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. and Clobazam 10 mg nocte. He reports mild irritability on Levetiracetam that is tolerable and denies current rash, visual disturbance, or significant gastrointestinal symptoms. No recent hospital admissions. He does not drive.

Examination today was unremarkable with normal cognition, intact cranial nerves, and no focal neurological deficits. Weight and metabolic observations are stable. Previous EEG demonstrated generalised spike‑and‑wave discharges consistent with a generalised epilepsy syndrome; MRI brain (last year) was normal.

Plan: We discussed ongoing self‑management and recording of events. Given the present pattern of ≤ four seizures per week and acceptable tolerability, he prefers to continue current therapy for now while we complete repeat EEG with hyperventilation and photic stimulation to re‑assess generalised activity. I have arranged this for the next available slot and requested medication levels and routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs) to ensure no biochemical contributors. We also reviewed safety, seizure first aid, and avoidance of identified precipitants. He is aware of SUDEP information resources. Follow‑up is planned in 4 months, with earlier review if the frequency increases or new adverse effects occur.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: a frequency of ≤ four seizures per week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the overall seizure frequency: '≤ four seizures per week.' No other numeric frequencies contradict or exceed this. Event types are described but without separate quantified rates. As per the rule to select the highest frequency, we use the stated overall frequency. The allowed format requires numerals or numeric ranges; '≤ four' is best normalized to a range '0 to 4', but since the patient is not seizure-free and is currently having seizures, we represent the upper bound as '4 per week' to reflect the stated frequency ceiling.
seizure_frequency_number: a frequency of ≤ four seizures per week
